text: The surgeon inserts a myoma screw into the fibroid and uses a monopolar instrument to make a sharp incision along the posterior wall at the fibroid's most prominent part. The monopolar is employed to enucleate the deep intramural fibroid from its bed, dissecting it out of its capsule with gentle traction and counter traction. The fibroid is separated from its vascular bed, and fine attachments are removed with minimal bleeding due to prior bilateral uterine artery coagulation.

The surgeon sutures the fibroid capsule back in multiple layers using a barbed suture, which eliminates the need for knot tying and expedites the procedure. Deep interrupted sutures are placed through the myoma cavity.

Using a suprapubic port and two needle holders, the surgeon performs deep suturing through the myoma bed, minimizing hematoma formation. Continuous barbed sutures are applied to close the cavity, with minimal bleeding due to prior uterine artery coagulation. Monopolar is used for a sharp cut at the myoma level, ensuring smooth approximation of the uterine serosa.

After completing the suturing with barbed sutures, the surgeon uses a morcellator to remove the fibroid from the peritoneal cavity. An extra stitch is taken to interlock with previous stitches, ensuring tension is maintained along the suture line. The fibroid is enucleated and extracted in long strips using the opposite port as a guide.
